dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 15 coming in for breathing difficulty with shortness of breath. Female patient age 56 in Richmond.
Hi there, I'm Alex, I'm an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 tonight?
I'm having really bad shortness of breath. It started earlier in the evening and has gradually gotten worse.
Are you awake right now and able to follow directions?
Can you open your eyes and tell me where you are?
Yes, I'm in Richmond. I know what's happening.
If I clap my hands, will you raise your hands too? Once you hear it, do it.
(Patient hears the clap and raises both hands.)
Can I check your airway? I need to make sure it's open without obstruction.
Okay.
Let's take a look at your breathing. Can I place a pulse oximetry clip on your finger?
Yes.
Respirations: 33, tachypneic, mild wheezing heard in upper and lower right fields. Breathing seems a little labored.
SpO2 is 95% on room air now. You're already on home oxygen via a nasal cannula.
May I check your pulse and blood pressure?
Sure.
Pulse is 116, regular, and strong throughout.
Blood pressure is 210 over 106.
Pulse ox is now 93%.
We were unable to get a reading for glucose. We'll proceed with an IV for potential medication needs.
I'm going to listen to your breathing to check for sounds and air movement.
Yes, go ahead.
There's mild wheezing in upper and lower right fields. You're not pale or diaphoretic at the moment.
Can you tell me when the shortness of breath began and how it's felt?
It started earlier in the evening and has gradually worsened.
You're currently using home oxygen through a nasal cannula, correct?
Yes.

dialogue.role: events leading up to the event; Paramedic
dialogue.utterance: What caused the worsening breathing? Did you have any trigger like illness or missed meds?

dialogue.role: events leading up to the event; Patient
dialogue.utterance: I didn't have any of my medication with me and couldn't manage symptoms at home.
Are you currently experiencing any chest discomfort or pain? Any other pain besides shortness of breath?
There was some generalized chest discomfort initially, but it's gone now.
When did you first notice shortness of breath?
Not exactly sure-has gotten worse gradually this evening.
Have you noticed anything that worsens or improves your breathing?
Using my home oxygen helps a little, but it's still hard to breathe.
On a scale of 1 to 10, how severe would you say the shortness of breath is now?
Pretty severe, like a 9-worse than some COPD flares in the past, but not the worst.
Has any discomfort spread from your chest to other areas like your arms, neck, or back?
No, it was only in my chest.
Have you experienced any headache, dizziness, abdominal pain, nausea, or vomiting?
No headache, dizziness, abdominal pain, nausea, or vomiting.
Are you allergic to any medications?
I'm allergic to aspirin.
May I ask what medications do you usually take?
I take amlodipine, Lantus, Humalog, Atorvastatin, Protonix, Gabapentin, Clonidine, Coreg, Hydroxychloroquine, Lovenox, Phenergan, and Fluoxetine.
Do you have inhalers for breathing issues like Albuterol or Atrovent?
I usually rely on those, but I don't have them with me now.
What is your medical history?
I have COPD, diabetes, hypertension, lupus, kidney disease, hyperkalemia, anemia, anxiety, and depression.
Attempting IV access in your arm now.
Initiating nebulizer therapy with Albuterol and Atrovent.
Administering 5mg Albuterol and 0.5mg Atrovent via nebulizer. This should help open your airways.
Completed nebulizer treatment. Oxygen remains on via nasal cannula.
Attaching a 4-lead EKG belt now. We'll do a full 12-lead EKG soon after.
We're following protocol 3-11 for COPD exacerbation and respiratory distress.
Reassessing: Pulse is now 109. Slight improvement noted.
Blood pressure remains elevated at 208 over 107.
Breathing remains tachypneic at 33, no significant change yet.
Pulse ox improved to 99% post nebulizer.
EKG now shows sinus tachycardia without STE or ectopy.
Re-checking your breathing. Some improvement in wheezing, but tachypnea still present.
Do you have a preferred hospital, or shall I suggest one?
Yes, I would like to go to VCU Health Systems.
Preparing for transfer using stand and pivot method, and securing you with straps.
You're securely loaded and strapped on the stretcher. All transport setups are complete.
Transport begins now. I will monitor your vitals continuously.
En route vitals: 208/107, 109, 33, 99%, sinus tachycardia. EtCO2 is normal at 28.
No new treatments needed during transport. Your vitals are stable.
No significant change in breathing severity during the ride.
All medications and equipment on board. We're ready for the handover to the ED.
Arriving at VCU Health Systems now. ED has been informed of our arrival.
Transferring care to the emergency department. All ePCR and vital data have been shared.
We're receiving the care now. Handover complete.
All care has been transferred to the hospital team.
No further care by our team is needed. Handoff has been completed.
ED team will now manage your care. We're calling this incident concluded.
Provided with full med history and vitals. Patient is en route.
Pulse ox improved to 99% post nebulizer. Better than pre-treatment readings.
Oxygen saturation remains at 99%-stable during transport.
Checking for rashes or skin changes. No findings observed as per report.
BP remains elevated but stable. Noting results for the ED.
Systolic BP stayed in high 200s throughout. Will be flagged in ED.
No appreciable improvement in symptoms yet. No new pain reported.
No additional medications or treatments needed en route.
With the EKG results, we'll be setting up a cardiac workup as a precaution.
Agreed-will be informing them of the 12-lead and field interpretation.
Final vitals en route show stability in SpO2, BP, and pulse.
Comprehensive list of medications was given, including Lovenox and Coreg. ED is aware.
Rechecking respiration rate-still at 33. Saturation improved post nebulizer.
Appropriate documentation was completed. Handoff smooth.
EPCR timestamped for each significant action taken in the call.
After landing at the hospital, we'll do a full debrief and send updates to the system.
Your BP is chronically high. ED will closely monitor and document that.
No new issues encountered in transport. Stable vitals and no new complaints.
Your lack of asthma meds may have triggered this, based on info presented by you.
Your long-term issues like anxiety and depression were relayed for ED awareness.
Full medical list with dosages on record. ED will be reviewing them now.
No improvement in breath with home oxygen reported. Expect this to continue.
No family present-as such, moved you with full protocol followed.
Transport continued until ED, and you were immediately handed over.
All vital monitoring tools left with you per our procedure.
ED checked your vital signs and reviewed medical history. Transfer clear.
EKG transmitted in the field-ED will do their own if needed.
Vital signs updated, all post-nebulizer. Pulse ox is at 99%.
No vomiting, dizziness, or new pain-everything aligned with protocol.
This was a fast-ramped call but with no significant changes in condition.
You take 10 meds, but were missing albuterol and atrovent tonight.
Good for you to follow up with your pulmonary and PCP as soon as possible.
This call escalated due to COPD and cardiac risk. Glad you reached ED safely.
Chronic issues were well-documented so ED can take it from here.
